Welcome back to Getting Started, Lessons with the Masters, as Clayton and Tony continue their conversation by helping you master the absolutely crucial skill of time management with a twist. You see, direct marketing often gives you more freedom than ever before, but also more temptation, and your overall productivity and success will depend largely upon how you use or abuse that freedom. Listen in as Clayton and Tony reveal the strategies that have placed them among the most effective pros in the business. Now let's listen in. Hey Tony. Hey Clayton. Alright, I'm ready for you. Okay, cool. We are calling this session Freedom, Temptation, and Time Management. I just want to preface the issue. Anybody who's been a writer, especially a freelance writer for a period of time, has experienced a sudden boom in terms of freedom in their schedule and their lives, but also a huge increase in temptation and the necessity for more of a system and a way of dealing with that freedom so they can get a lot of writing done. As you said before, you're only really making money when you're writing. Making sure that you're doing your very best to get what you need to get done done and then enjoying your free time and bringing balance to your life. That's what this is about. I know that you were considered to be one of the most productive people in the industry. When I talk to guys like Paris and others, they're just like, oh, how much you're able to get done. I've been here and seen you obviously get quite a bit done too. I think everybody's excited to get your secrets in this area. Thank you for that Tony. I kind of did it by necessity. I started first of all, from the time I was a little boy, my dad just literally beat work ethic into me. The fact that the world doesn't know you living, that you have to earn your own way in the world, when you fall on hard times, it's because of decisions you've made and learned from those decisions and move on and work your own way out of problems. From my earliest days, I remember, Saturdays were not for fun. They were for mowing the lawn and picking the apples off the crab apple tree and doing all of the errands around the house that we had to do. Work was just a part of life. Later on, when I got into this racket, copywriting, I was married and I had two kids and my wife didn't work. It was up to me to keep the family fed. In those early days, when I first went out on my own, I worked for an ad agency for several years as a copywriter. I really got my chops there. When I left around 1979 or 80, I was basically sitting there with no paycheck, no money coming in and an overhead. I had rent. I had to buy food. Getting up every morning with no money in the bank, with no money supposedly coming to me in the mail and with a family looking to me to provide for them, I had all the incentive I needed to go to work and to find ways to solve problems and resist the temptation to loathe rather than to yield to them. Having said that, I find procrastination is one of the greatest joys in life. The whole idea of thinking about this big thing, I have to do this mind-bending amount of work I have to do. Then at the last minute, deciding I'm going to give myself a day off, that's one of the great pleasures of life. I had to learn early on though, and it wasn't hard to learn. A couple of bounce checks will generally do it for you. I had to grow up and I had to be a man about this. I was a provider for a family. I had to look for ways to break those habits or to resist temptation and just go to work and do the work. One of the things that it seems like I've learned from you is that discipline is, in a lot of ways, discipline is freedom. I think even when you talk about procrastination, you generally do it within the context of you're not doing it randomly. A lot of times, like you mentioned, when you first get a promotion, you first take on a project, you'll go through the research. You kind of force yourself to go through the research or go through at least one part of the assignment. Then you'll procrastinate, like the writing part or another element of it. By doing that, you can actually enjoy ... You know you've done one thing that's necessary and then you can actually enjoy your time. I think a lot of writers, we get stuck in this gray area where we're not fully doing the work or something, actually being productive or effective. Then when we're procrastinating, we're kind of thinking about work all the time. We're in this zone that's not really enjoyable or effective on either side. We don't retard or rejuvenate or ... Well, it's miserable. If there's something that you know you should be doing and you know there's a deadline looming and you know that there are people counting on you to deliver a promotion and you just decide not to do it, then you're not going to really enjoy that time anyway because you feel guilty about it. You constantly are carrying the weight of that deadline around with you. There's something I call creative procrastination. When you first begin a project, you're going to get that care package that we've talked about from the client, all the things you've asked for. A sample of the product and the premiums, the testimonials, the competitors promos, past promos this client has, maybe the control that you're supposed to beat. There's a lot of reading that needs to be done and it comes in a very large envelope or a box and it's pretty intimidating. Nine times out of ten, it arrives just as you're completing another project and there's a temptation to just kind of leave it on your desk unopened and be like Scarlett O'Hara. I'll think about that tomorrow. Then when you finally do have time, it's still sitting there, this large stack of stuff. Reading a year's worth of back issues of a semi-monthly financial newsletter is not exactly enjoyable reading, but you have to do it. You have to understand where the editor of the newsletter is coming from. You need to compile a track record of his past recommendations. There's a lot of work there to be done. For me, the reading part is the most boring part of the whole thing. I know I have to input that information into my brain, but the process of doing it is not one of the more fun things I do. That's true when I was doing book promotions for Boardroom. They'd send you this book you had to promote and it was 600 pages long and it was eight-point type and you had to work your way through that and write fascinations and proof elements and it's just backbreaking work. There is a temptation at that point, just who's going to know, who's going to care if I put it off for one more day? What I found is that the time away from the work, the time before you actually start writing, can be one of two things. It can either be that guilt-ridden time where you feel like you should be doing something but you just can't get up the oomph to tear into the material and start doing the in-depth research or you can take that time and really enjoy it. If first, you just dive in and read everything. You don't have to take copious notes. You can take some notes if you like but you don't have to take copious notes. You don't have to do any writing or really come up with anything. All you really need to do is to internalize the material that you've been sent. Nine times out of ten, that's going to suggest some ideas, possibly some headlines or a theme. The most important part of it is that you have started away in your subconscious brain. Your subconscious is like a filing cabinet. It doesn't have the capacity to reason or think. All it does is stores away material and then makes connections between that material and other material, other memories that it has or other facts or figures or other campaigns you've seen. Once you've put it in there, your subconscious brain is doing a ton of work for you. Anytime your conscious brain is out of the way and not actually thinking about the project, including at times when you're asleep, your subconscious brain is mulling it over, finding places to file it, finding connections between what you read and other things that you have filed away. Those connections are really the definition of creativity. Creativity has been defined by several people. I respect this being the capacity to connect to previously unrelated facts or ideas in a new way. Your subconscious brain is great at that. Even if you do take a day or two off and not actually begin writing, the work is being done. Actually, it's helpful to do just that. This has been written about a lot of the greatest geniuses in history. We're famous for, like Henry Ford, for example, with his rather Edison, with his famous naps. He even wrote about the fact that he would go to sleep with an intractable problem. When he woke up, the solution would be sitting there for him. You can see that. I play a lot of scrabble when I'm trying to wake up and kill time in the morning. I've noticed on several occasions, I'll be sitting there with a lousy rack of letters, and I can't for the life of me figure out how to use them. When I get up and go grab a cup of coffee or spend five minutes away from the computer and come back, sit down, and instantly I see the solution. It's just the way our brains work. Harnessing that is great. This is a great example of that on the movie, Alma Deis. I've heard it first heard on a Gene Schwartz call. He mentioned that. It's basically, Mozart is obviously a genius anyway, but when he was composing music, he would actually write a line and then he would roll a billiard ball across the table. It would engage his conscious mind in a different way and allow his subconscious to process the notes and what was going on. The next line would come to him and he would write it. That was part of why he was famous for never needing to revise, never having to ... Everything was perfect the first time. He had a very set process for shifting back and forth between conscious and subconscious and it worked beautifully. I don't know if we can ever come to that point where we never need to revise anything. I came fairly close one time and I don't do this anymore because my days tend to be fairly hectic. I told the story of when I wrote the first launch promo for Health and Healing Newsletter. I had just written, previously that I'd written a promotion for another health newsletter. It was the first time I'd ever written a promotion for a health newsletter and it was for a newsletter called Cardiac Alert that Phil was publishing put out. Up to that time, I was only writing financial promos. I wrote this promotion for a health cardiac alert and it bombed. It was just an absolute abysmal bomb. There was no market for the letter. It was mainstream medical advice. Of course, it hadn't occurred to anybody to do an alternate health newsletter at that point but it was a cardiologist. His name was Dr. Jorge Rios and he was giving out all of the typical standard mainstream establishment advice for heart health. The promotion bombed. I'm sitting there going, I never want to write one of these things again. It was a total waste of time for me. Then they called me up and asked me if I'd write another health promotion. I told them I really didn't want to but they talked me into it. I hated every minute of it. I thought, what a freaking waste of time. How stupid am I? What a wuss. I should have just said no but instead I was trying to please the client so I accepted the assignment. I wanted to tear through it as quickly as I could to get onto some stuff that would actually make me money. I flew out to California and met with a doctor, Dr. Whitaker, for a couple hours in his office. I had read his material. He had written a book or two and some newspaper articles. I read that material and I had grabbed a couple of other books on home remedies, not the Rodale book that hadn't come out yet but other books on home remedies and I read them on the plane. The next day I just went to the—I got up in the morning at my hotel in California and I said, well, I was dreading writing this. I thought, well, I'll tell you what, I'm going to give myself an hour at the pool for every hour I—no, it was 15 minutes at the pool for every hour I write. I did that. I'd write for an hour and then I lay down at the pool, get some sun, take a quick swim. I had a candy laptop computer, I remember, and I took it down to the pool with me. We'd probably wait about 10 pounds at the time. Yeah, it should have had pastors on it. I just did that all day long. 15 minutes of rest for every hour of work. The great news is that that was one of the best promotions I've ever written. I really think the reason for it, first of all, as I was actually doing the work, that 15 minute break allowed my subconscious to catch up because I was already consciously thinking where I was going to go next. It gave my subconscious mind some time to make the necessary connections. But also, it speeded up the process and the quality of the work was great. When I came back, I had to fill in a few facts and figures that I'd been missing when I wrote it, but wow, it was just a great way to write. Yeah, well, most people don't—I mean, just to illustrate what a feat that is, I mean, most people probably don't realize that a maglog, you're talking about a 30 to 40-page word document of near-perfect copy written in a single day, it's very, very difficult. Most people take a month to do that. Sure. At least a week. Yeah, absolutely. And of course, at the same time, you're thinking about visuals, charts, graphs, cartoons, side characters. So, yeah, it really—and it's still possible. I mean, anybody can do this. Depending on where you are in your career, you may not find that you write a maglog in a day, but like you said, look, the top copywriters will all tell you it takes them a month to write a promo. And they'll all take two months, and in some cases, three months. You know, none of them have ever produced a better return than the 4% we got on that piece that was written in a day. So, you know, there's something about that process that really works. Yeah, you know, and one of the things that's really interesting about it, I wanted to relate that to another point in terms of this whole picture is that when you study time management, like there's a guru out right now that's talking a lot about it. He's addressing the internet marketing community. One of the things he mentions is that willpower is our most precious resource as humans, and that if you have to rely on willpower all the time, you're probably going to fail. If you have to wait until you feel like it, if you're going to will yourself to work every day, and what he says is that with the available willpower that you have, you should channel that into creating a routine or a procedure or some kind of ritual that you follow on an ongoing basis that you know works for you, and it's going to produce the results that you want, you know. That obviously what you experience there, even though that wasn't your strict routine forever, like it at least taught you kind of a process that you later on use. And I know that you've also, and to be in other places, you've talked about your personal schedule waking up at a certain time every morning of doing certain things in a certain order every day so that you don't have to think about it. You don't have to really will yourself to do it. It's just there and you can rely on it. And I think a lot of times that frees up the creative process too, because you're not fighting with yourself. You know, you're allowing things to flow and then it happens really. For me willpower is no longer part of the equation. I think what you're talking about basically is at the beginning using your willpower to create a habit. Exactly. You know, and I think I get up every morning at the same time, well fairly much same time, between one and four o'clock. Okay, and I go to bed at eight. I get up between one and four a.m. and it's habit. I try to sleep in. I can't do it. And I go to work and I always start with no pressure on me at all. You know, I'm groggy. I have to go down a flight of stairs from the master bedroom through the entire length of the house, out onto the porch, down an outside flight of railroad-tied stairs to cabin in the woods, all the time hoping that there's not a snake on the stair or a grizzly bear in the woods, which kind of wakes you up a little bit, because there have been snakes on the stairs. And there have been bears in our trash. And go down to this little cedar cabin I've got and make a pot of coffee and sit down and check my email. And you know, for the first half hour, I don't feel any pressure at all to do anything but wake up. But that time is precious to just awaken for the day, because again, your subconscious has been working all night. So then I open the document I'm working on and I don't give myself any pressure to do anything with it, just to look at it. And you know, for any writer, you know, they say that the most powerful human urge is not sex or food or water, but it's the urge that one person has to change another person's copy. It's the same thing with me changing my own copy. You know, I'll be looking at what I wrote yesterday and I'll be scrolling down through it and I'll see a word that was misused or a typo or a comma is missing and I'll just, you know, do one little click to fix it and then I'll go a little farther and then I'll notice a phrase that isn't quite right. And before you know it, I'm full blown editing. You know, and so, you know, I kind of seduce myself into going to work that way. You know, the Chinese saying that a journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step, you know, it's true, it's trite, but it's absolutely true. I mean, you have to do the very first thing, which is open the envelope when it arrives, the very second thing, which is to take the material out, the very third thing, which is to begin reading, but it's also, you know, it's also true from the other standpoint. If you were sitting there in a nice comfortable home thinking about having to walk a thousand miles, it would be very intimidating and all you could think about is the tremendous amount of effort and discomfort and inconvenience that you're going to have to go through to get through that thousand mile trip. But if, you know, all you're thinking about is one step, you know, then it's easy. I'm big on compartmentalization, you know, and I've kind of had to become a compartmentalizer because I don't necessarily have an organized mind. I have friends that can handle 50 different issues a day. You know, they can write a little bit on this, edit that, review copy on this, you know, hundreds of little tasks a day, and each time they have to shift gears, and they can do that, and they can do it quickly and efficiently. It takes me time to get my mind back into a project once I've been interrupted. And so compartmentalizing and saying, for the next hour, I'm just going to do this. It's all I expected myself. That's all I'm going to do. And nine times out of ten, you surprise yourself because you thought you were just going to, you know, read the first ten pages of the research kit. But in reality, as you're reading them, you had five ideas that you put down in your note document. So, you know, it's taking it in bite-sized pieces and enjoying the work. You're not going to enjoy it if you're, you know, inside each of us, they're supposedly, you know, the psychiatrist's posts are two people. Okay, there's the adult and there's the child. And the adult is usually like a prototype of our fathers, you know. Ah, you're no damn good. You're a lazy bum. Get up off the couch. Go do the work. Do how you didn't do that work. Right. Do it over again. You'll never amount to anything. You suck, you know. And you know, constantly berating us, correcting us, questioning our intentions or our morals or our abilities or talents. And then there's a little kid inside us that just wants to tell that guy to get lost and go play. In fact, they say that that's what alcohol does. It kills the adult. And, yeah. And so, you know, so the kid can come out and play. And so, you know, when you're doing this work, if you're, you know, if your adult is telling you, you're a bomb, you've procrastinated too long or you'll never get through this, it's way too long. There's way too much to do. This is way too hard for you. Now, if you're constantly getting that kind of message, you know, from your own mind, then the work isn't fun. But once you learn to tell that guy to just shut the hell up and get him to shut up by having a process where you can feel good about the work that you do every day, that you've taken a big step forward, then, you know, you're nine-tenths of the way there. That's a great point. I really love, like, one of the things that I've thought about, you know, and observed in terms of beating procrastination was to just, like, you know, in sales, they say, just get, if you want to make more sales, just get in front of more prospects. I think that if you want, from a copywriting perspective, if you want to write more, just get in front of the copy more. Like, it's as simple just like you said, just sit down and even if you just stare at the page and don't even necessarily force or require that you do anything, but just getting in front of it, somehow there's like this magical thing that happens and you start to write. Absolutely. You know, that's what I do in the mornings. And again, not expecting anything of myself other than just open the document and take a look at what I did yesterday. And it's just a very natural process. You're going to want to start making improvements. You're going to see things you didn't see. There'll be ideas that your subconscious came up with overnight that you'll be able to implement. It's a natural way of doing it. It's not forced. That's great. That's great. And one of the things that, you know, you also come up from what you said, I think, is a direct response. Marketers, you know, we're obviously familiar on a copy perspective from, you know, with the process of testing, optimizing, looking at different variations of something and then picking the one that's working the best. I think that with this time management stuff and routine and scheduling and working, that there's also a tremendous opportunity to do that. I think that because everybody's different, you know, what works for you is not necessarily going to work for somebody else. Somebody might be more of a night owl. They might need to work at, they might work best at a coffee shop. But I think that if you sincerely try different things, you kind of, you look at the results and essentially you're testing out different things and then you're seeing what produces the best results from you and then that becomes your control, what you continue to do. You may at some point test other aspects of a routine or work schedule or habit, you know, against that. And if you can improve upon it, you do. And if you can't, you just go with what's working best. But I think not a lot of people do that. I think the pages, especially beginning writers, because they're just, they don't have that understanding yet. So I think that that is a really, really good way to look at it and to know that they have the freedom to try a bunch of things and really to look at what's working best. I think that's the beauty of the profession that we're in is that you don't have a boss standing over our shoulder. We can work from anywhere. We can work for as long or as little as we want at a time. And so we do have the opportunity to really try out what works and then refine and optimize that over time. Well, yeah. And also, you know, again, yeah, I want to make a clear differentiation here. You know, where I think the habits come in are not on the work level. It's on the life level. I think, you know, if the habits, the work for me are the ones that have to do with time I get up, what my morning routine is, you know, those kinds of things. And that gets me to my desk. Okay. And you're right. I mean, depending on who you are, the times of day you may write, you know, find most productive for yourself will differ. Although I highly recommend trying early mornings. No interruptions. You've just awoken from a good night's sleep. And you're fresh. And also, that's when your mind is most creative. So I highly recommend early mornings. But each job that I do basically tells me what my process will be on that job. You know, there'll be some jobs where I'll need to read a 600-page book. And while I'm doing that, you know, plowing through all of those pages, I'm going to have to write rough fascinations on value the book delivers. You know, when you're writing a fascination package, there are two things you're looking through when you're going through. Number one, you're looking for factoids or tips in that book. Where if you revealed what the tip is, people would say, well, who doesn't know that? But by saying, by posing this fascination, you create fascination and intriguing people are dying to know what the answer is. Like Paris' famous fascination, how a pickpocket cured my aching back. Well, that was from a tip in a book, a boardroom book, where the tip was that many people find that if they have a large wallet and spend a lot of time sitting each day, that that wallet is the cause of their back problems. And so by simply removing the wallet, they're able to cure their back problems. Well, how boring would that be to say in a promotion? But instead, you know, Paris found a way to present the fact that that fact was in the book in an intriguing way where I just absolutely had to buy the book to find out how in a world a pickpocket was going to cure my back. The other thing that you're looking for as you're going through that book is you're looking for just the opposite. You're looking for factoids that are forehead slappers. You're looking for things that are so amazing that, you know, you want to give them away in a promotion. And that becomes a credibility element or a proof element where you're demonstrating just how cool this book is that has facts in it like this, you know. And one of them, for example, I remember was evidently when EMT professionals call for an ambulance, they say send the wagon. And the test had shown that if you call 911 and instead of saying help, send an ambulance, you say send the wagon. The response time was like 10 minutes faster on average. So wow, you know, how did they run life during a heart attack? How to get the ambulance there 10 minutes faster? And then have a paragraph that actually delivers that piece of information in the promo creates tremendous readership, credibility, proof element of how cool this book is really makes you want to order it. So those are the two things you're looking for as you're writing a boardroom style package, right? As you're reading, as you're doing your first read. Well, that's a completely different process than what I go through when I'm writing a promotion for an investment newsletter or a health newsletter or a supplement, you know, or any other product. So you need to let the project itself tell you how it wants to get done. And so, you know, the habits apply to how you live your life, but don't feel that you have to impose them on each job and do each job the same way. Yeah, that's a great point. And I think one of the biggest things that I've learned from you, especially in this area, is, you know, when I first, when I first wasn't here physically, you know, it was very intimidated. And because I was here, you were talking about how you worked 12, 16, 18, I guess sometimes even 20 hours a day. And when I got here, I realized you don't always do that. You know, there's some days when you leave at 12, you know, although you may have been working eight hours before most people, you know, if they left at 12, they may have been working three or four hours. But the point is, is that you just do whatever it takes to get that particular day's work or that particular job's work done, and no more, no less, you know, always. And that, like you're saying, it's always dictated by the job. You know, if you need, you have to put in 18 hours in, you put 18 hours in. If you have to put in two hours in, just write a couple of emails that day, that's what you do. And I think that's such a fantastic mindset. I don't know how exactly you came to really develop that. I know you said, you have good work coming from your family. But if there's any other thing about that you could share in terms of just the doing it whatever it takes to get something done, kind of mentality that you develop, because I think that that's a priceless thing that other people would love to be able to actually apply. Part of that is from wanting the paycheck as sooner you get the work done, the sooner you get paid, and also giving yourself incentives along the way. You know, as soon as I get the stunning, I'm going to give myself three days off to go do whatever I want. I'm going to race my motorcycle or my car. I'm going to go do a project in the yard. I'm going to do something that I've been wanting to do. I'm going to Vegas. I'm going to take the redhead to a spa, you know, but give yourself some incentive. Another part of it though really is, and this just occurred to me while you were asking the question, I really see every project I work on as really three projects. Before each project is having three phases. You know, there's the first burst of creativity, actually four, because before that there's a research phase. But as you're actually writing, the first phase really is a burst of creativity. That's where you're connecting all of the dots, you're putting all of these ideas together that you got from the outside research and from studying the product and the market and the competition and so forth. You're starting to conceive the promotion. What's it going to look like? What's it going to feel like? Is it going to be a story lead, a benefit headline lead, a topic lead? What kind of lead would really work best with this? So it's all very creative kind of work. Then the second part of the work, and you go through the entire, it's not just the lead, it's the entire project like that, because everything you're doing on that first rough draft is creative because it's never existed before. You're creating it out of a whole cloth. And so that's a creative phase. Then the second phase is kind of like a jigsaw puzzle phase. The second phase is where you go in and you go, okay, all right, well I've shot all my creative bullets on this. Okay, I've had all of these great ideas and I spent the last hours or days or whatever getting that done. Now it's time to go back and look at how this thing unfolds, the order, its sequence, right? And that's the time where you basically pull it all apart and then put it back together in an order that makes more sense and it's more compelling and it keeps the prospect reading. Okay, now that's fun. I mean people spend their Sunday mornings doing crossword puzzles and the vacation is doing jigsaws. I mean that's challenging, it's fun. But then the last phase I really hate and that's going through what I now have as a complete draft that's pretty darn good and going through and doing the fine work because I am not a detail-oriented person. And so it's a chore. I have to go through and I have to make sure the numbers are all there and that the facts are right and that the grammar is correct and that the paragraphs are an average of five lines long and so the page won't look too forbidding. That my subheads are structured correctly. The presentation is such that the client will be able to read it and get what's the subheads, what's an indented paragraph, where the bullets go, where the sidebars go and all of that stuff so I can make the final presentation to the client. And frankly that bore the living daylights out of me. And so the whole time I'm doing that, what's getting me through that, what got me through the creative part was it was fun. It got me through the jigsaw puzzle part, that was fun too. What gives me through that final process is as soon as I get through here I'm going to order a pizza. Or I'm going to go have a drink or I'm going to take the redhead to sushi or something like that. And it'll be over and I'll get to send an invoice. So different parts of the job come with their own rewards and it helps me to keep those rewards in the forefront of my mind. That's awesome. And one of the things that that really helps me realize as a writer I think is that we've talked about this a lot obviously from a promotional side that people buy and act on emotions. They don't really, they justify later on logic but that's not the impulse that drives the action. And I think it's the same a lot of times even as much as we hate to realize it's the same a lot of times with work and to the extent that we obviously it's, there's a million and one reason it's not to procrastinate but those are all logical reasoning and arguments and doesn't necessarily always, we could be perfectly fluent in that reasoning and still procrastinate all the time. It doesn't necessarily always change our behavior. So giving yourself emotional reasons and incentives to, you know, to get to sit down to your desk or to get the work done or to finish a task is so important because that's really the only way that you're going to get yourself to act. And I think habit bypasses emotions too, which is, which I mean it bypasses like the whole logical process. But I think like you're saying incentivizing yourself emotionally to sit down to do a task, do the work, figuring out whatever emotional state you need and what you need to promise yourself or set up or what kind of, you know, to get yourself there is a really, really unique thing but it's a crucial thing I think. Absolutely. Yeah. Yeah. So if you're going through the piece, you know, there's, we think in layers, it's almost like, you know, those photographs where a guy is in a hall of mirrors and there's an infinite number of him as his image is reflected in the mirrors but it's the same way with kind of how we live our lives. And you know, there's being in the moment, which is just basically unaware of yourself only being aware of the world around you, right? And that's a great place to be. But then there's another layer above that, which is watching yourself in a situation and being keenly aware of what you're doing and what you're moving through and almost like you're, you know, outside your own body and above yourself watching yourself do the work. And that's the state that I have to be in when I'm thinking, okay, as a prospect's reading this, what's the appropriate emotion? What's the appropriate emotional tone for this copy? You know, and so you're right. I mean, different mindsets for different tasks. Yeah, definitely. Well, last thing, let's see, we can end this on a fun note. I think one of the challenges, like I mentioned in the beginning, what I just wanted to touch on a little bit more is that a lot of writers, like I mentioned, we get in this gray zone where we don't enjoy it, we set things up so we don't necessarily enjoy the writing process a lot of times. And then also we're feeling guilty or we're not in a place where we're enjoying our free time. And I think part of the problem there is that we need to be clear. I think you wanted to keep your success now looking at you is that you know what recharges you and makes you feel better when you're not writing so that you actually have energy and excitement when you come back to work. And I think a lot of people when they're procrastinating or they're not doing the work that they need to do, they're doing like email or other stuff that's not rejuvenating them in any way. And then coming back to the writing and the work is more difficult. So yeah, if you could just talk a little bit about that, I think that would be very helpful for people. Sure. I don't know if this is where you're going on that, but basically, you know, I've always believed that life needs to be balanced. And in fact, within our own personalities, you know, we were talking about having the father and the child. Obviously if the father was dominant and there was no child, no desire to play, we would be very deadly, serious, unhappy people. And on the other hand, if the child within us was allowed free range without the restrictions, the constraints that the adult within us exerts on him, then we would be extremely unproductive and pretty much worthless human beings. So it's a balance of the two that makes it work. And in a very similar way, each one of us has two aptitudes. You know, we have a creative aptitude and we have an accounting aptitude, right brain, left brain. And one of the things that I've found over the years is that when I'm doing only creative work, I finally get to a point of saturation, you know, where I'm not being creative anymore. And when I'm doing just detail-oriented work, you know, I get to a point where I'm just absolutely brain dead. I couldn't add two plus two if you held a gun to my head. And because we all have these aptitudes, we all have a need to exercise these aptitudes. And if a particular aptitude hasn't been exercised for a period of time, let's say for example, your detail-oriented left brain accounting aptitude hasn't been exercised for a while, then it's going to start raising its hands and blocking the creative aptitude. And so I quite often find that if I'm at a point where I'm crunching numbers like on a track record or, you know, doing very detail-oriented work, that when I get to the point where I feel like my head's going to explode if I have to do one more of those detail-oriented things, getting away from that work and then turning my attention to the creative work works really well. And see, the alternative is what most people do is they'll be doing a particular type of work, either right brain or left brain, and they'll get to the point where they're just absolutely played out. They're toast, and they say, you know, that's it for me, that's all I can do today. And they don't realize that it's just that aptitude that they've exhausted. And there are still four or five or six or ten more productive hours in the day, and those hours are going to be wasted because you think you're played out, but it's only that one aptitude that is. And so if you turn to the other, if you were doing detail-oriented stuff, left brain stuff, you turn to the other aptitude, you start exercising it for a while, you're going to find that it's very active, and you're reinvigorated, re-energized. And depending on which type of work is needed the most, you know, you may just go to creative stuff for an hour and then return to the detail-oriented stuff, or you might spend the next several hours on the creative stuff. I've found that it could be as simple for me sometimes if I'm doing all creative stuff. I'm trying to write great headline, or I'm trying to come up with a new format for a direct mail package, something that's really out of the box, and I've really been struggling with it and going at it. And I finally just hit the wall. I find that something as simple as adding up a long column of four-digit numbers will exercise my accounting left brain aptitude enough to free me within a few minutes to go back to the creative task. So that's a great way to get more productive hours out of the day. Yeah, that's an awesome point. You really took it further than I was initially asking, which is great. I think that what I was also talking about there, I think, was also making sure that for writers, I think it's really important in making sure that what you do in your free time is bringing you a lot of joy and rejuvenation and recovery. Like, it's really, it's genuine recovery time. It's not, you're not spending time on, you know, like you go out and write your Harley or you do things that you really love so that when it comes or go to Vegas or the Indian reservation or things that you know that are going to completely rejuvenate you and make you excited to actually come back to work. And I think that the moral of the story is, yeah, for everybody that's different, and I think that you've just done a great job of figuring out what that is for you and for the new writers listening to this, I think that the task is, you know, to find that for himself or herself so that, you know, it's creating that same positive effect on their life and in their schedule and in their routine. Yeah, absolutely. I think the key is, look, I mean, the writer's life can be pretty lonely, especially if you're just freelancing. You lock yourself in a room for hours and hours at a time. You have very little human contact. And you know, when you're outside, when you're done, you know, you need, again, this is all about balance, you know, this is about having a balanced life. So you need to make up for the fact that you've been deprived of human contact for that period of time, you know, or, you know, in my case, it's confining. I'm inside four walls. Okay. And best freedom I know, best sense of freedom I know is to jump on a harlango, tear-assing across the mountains. So you know, it's, you know, it's really a journey of self-discovery. You need to be aware, you need to be, you know, have a well-defined view of who you are, what your tendencies are, talents are, what your shortcomings are, what your failures are, and offset those in some ways. But it's so hard for a lot of us to really look at ourselves critically and honestly, you know. And I mean, I had the experience last year where my, this girl, a little cutie pie, her name was Kathy England, and she was my little brother's first girlfriend when he was in seventh grade. And she had Googled my name and found the total package. And we've, we hadn't talked in, you know, since 1967 or something, you know. And she's a PhD in psychiatry and she has, you know, she teaches out in the UC University California system now. And she's written several books and a real success. And anyway, she called me up and we were just talking and laughing about old times. And she asked me a question, I forget what it was, and I answered and she goes, oh, well, you know your problem. You're a D.D.H.D. And that had never occurred to me before. Okay. But the fact of the matter is that, you know, I get like bored, crazy bored if I have to wait for something for five minutes. You know, I mean, I have to be mentally occupied all the time. And I hadn't realized about that about myself. And I, you know, I was 55 years old when she made that phone call. And in the month since, it's been great because I've been able to say, well, you know, of course I'm acting this way now because I always have to be occupied and let me focus on not being occupied. Just, you know, opening my mind, freeing my mind, taking some deep breaths and just relaxing. Because you know, I need that too. And so anyway, it's a journey of self-discovery. Just finding out what it is you need and then providing it for yourself. And like Tony, like you pointed out, Tony, that for me the confinement is offset by motorcycle riding. Okay. The, I don't know why I'm into speed. I haven't really, you know, why fast cars and motorcycles. But obviously that's fulfilling some need that isn't being fulfilled when I'm sitting there at work. And there are a lot of guys, I haven't motivated grass since the 1980s, but there are a lot of guys that find tremendous release in just doing yard work, just puttering, you know. But whatever it is for you that, you know, really recharges your batteries, I think the key is to not consciously be thinking about work during that period of time. Have something that occupies your mind in ways so that you're allowing your brain again to do the work, unobstructed by your conscious mind, allowing your subconscious to be chewing on the challenges that you were dealing with when you got up from your desk. Yeah. And for me, I actually, I totally agree with what you're saying. And actually I took up, for that very reason, I took up guitar this year and it's been great. Yeah, because I think, especially when there's like a physical activity, it's almost impossible for you to bring your work into it. Like something like an instrument, playing an instrument requires your focus, it's physical, your hands are moving, you're listening, it's kind of, it's capturing all of your senses and involving all of your senses. And it's like on a Harley, I mean you can't think about work while you're playing your Harley, you're probably going to fly off the cliff. So, you know, I think finding things like that, yes, is a great point. Yeah. Well, I think we've covered everything and there's been a lot of really tremendous information for anybody that wants to ignite, you know, their, the efficiency of their work process while I think enjoying also the amount of, the free time that they do have enjoying it more. So, I want to thank you, Clayton, I think it's been a good call. Good, well, I hope it helps. Okay. Thank you, Tony.
